coredata.dc:title: Straightforward paper sensors for the detection of SSRI drugs using tyrosine functionalized GQDs: Fluorescence ‘turn-off’ turns on the crucial dosage monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Ravi, Pavithra Verthikere
Maharajan, Abirami
Pattabiraman, Ajay
Pichumani, Moorthi
coredata.dc:description: All socioeconomic classes are impacted by the COVID-19 epidemic, with stress being one of the biggest symptoms causing indigestion, breathlessness, heart disease, obesity, and many more. The most commonly used selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) drugs include, escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine, and fluoxetine are provided by doctors. If the medicines are not taken in the recommended dosage, at the correct intervals, which can occasionally be fatal because persons with depression are susceptible to mood fluctuations. There are no appropriate ways to monitor high dosages in clinical laboratories, the requirement for simple, novel material with cutting-edge technology to do so is essential. Herein, we report the application of a single fluorescent probe, Tyrosine-GQDs for the detection of SSRI drugs. Upon addition of SSRI drugs into the Tyrosine-GQDs, the fluorescence emission intensity of Tyrosine-GQDs decreased due to the complex formation through H-bonding which is evidenced by the rise of new peaks in fluorescence spectra and lifetime measurements. The calculated detection limit proves that the designed nanoprobe is highly sensitive and selective towards escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine, and fluoxetine are 0.13 × 10−6 M, 0.15 × 10−6 M, 0.07 × 10−6 M, and 0.07 × 10−6 M, respectively. A paper-based fluorescent sensor is fabricated using the designed probe which is efficient in the detection of the dosage of SSRI drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tyrosine-GQDs
Functionalization
Nanoprobe
SSRI drugs
Antidepressants
Fluorescent paper sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of tyrosine functionalized GQDs
Quantification of the limit of detection, the limit of quantitation, and the quenching rate constant

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Pavithra V Ravi thanks the Council of Scientific & Industrial Research, India for the Senior Research Fellowship (08/757(0002)/2020EMR-I). The authors acknowledge The Management, SNR Sons Charitable Trust, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore, India for providing institutional research development fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of Tyrosine-GQDs using carbonization method.
a) XRD pattern, b) FTIR spectra, c) survey spectra, high-resolution spectra of d) carbon, e) nitrogen, and f) oxygen. Reprinted with permission from [47].
Characteristic fluorescence response of Tyrosine-GQDs (10 × 10−6 M) for each successive addition of the SSRI drugs (30 × 10−6 M) a) Esci, c) Serta, e) Pari and g) Flu.
H-bond formation between the Tyrosine-GQDs (10 × 10−6 M) and SSRI drugs (30 × 10−6 M).
Time-resolved fluorescence decay of Tyrosine-GQDs (10 × 10−6 M) in the absence and presence of SSRI drugs (30 × 10−6 M). The excitation wavelength is 398 nm.
The linear regression plot from which the limit of detection and quantification are calculated a) Esci, b) Serta, c) Pari, and d) Flu.

Characteristic fluorescence response of the complexes a) Tyrosine-GQDs∙Esci over the addition of S, P, and F; b) Tyrosine-GQDs∙Serta over the addition of P, F, and C; c) Tyrosine-GQDs∙Pari over the addition of C, S, and F; and d) Tyrosine-GQDs∙Flu over the addition of C, P, and S. Concentration of Tyrosine-GQDs and SSRI drugs used for the interference studies are 10 × 10−6 M and 30 × 10−6 M respectively. For simplification Esci, Serta, Pari, and Flu are denoted as C, S, P, and F in the figure.

Characteristic fluorescence response of Tyrosine-GQDs upon addition of SSIR drugs and their stability measurement with respect to time. Tyrosine-GQDs upon addition of (a) Esci, (b) Serta, (c) Pari, and (d) Flu.

Bar graph denoting the minimum time required for the complex formation and the trend of fluorescence emission changes with respect to time. Tyrosine-GQDs upon addition of (a) Esci, (b) Serta, (c) Pari, and (d) Flu.
The various concentrations of drug samples C, S, F, and P in sunlight and UV lamp of λ 365 nm.
Name and chemical structures of the SSRI drugs.
List of methods used for the detection of various SSRI Drugs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Limits of conventional methods used for the quantification of SSRI drugs.

row.entry: Complicated analysis procedures involve different organic solvents and additives. Time-consuming sample preparation
Applicable only for soluble mixture components
Construction and optimization of the transistor are required
Cost and limited sample throughput, lack of portability
An expensive and larger quantity of samples is required
Fabrication and optimization of the electrodes

, where, τ0 = Average lifetime; A = relative amplitude; T = lifetime; suffix i = number of exponential components.

head.ce:title: Straightforward paper sensors for the detection of SSRI drugs using tyrosine functionalized GQDs: Fluorescence ‘turn-off’ turns on the crucial dosage monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore 641 022, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore 641 022, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore 641 022, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore 641 022, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Government Primary Health Center, Anaikatti, Coimbatore 641 108, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Government Primary Health Center, Anaikatti, Coimbatore 641 108, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All socioeconomic classes are impacted by the COVID-19 epidemic, with stress being one of the biggest symptoms causing indigestion, breathlessness, heart disease, obesity, and many more. The most commonly used selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) drugs include, escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine, and fluoxetine are provided by doctors. If the medicines are not taken in the recommended dosage, at the correct intervals, which can occasionally be fatal because persons with depression are susceptible to mood fluctuations. There are no appropriate ways to monitor high dosages in clinical laboratories, the requirement for simple, novel material with cutting-edge technology to do so is essential. Herein, we report the application of a single fluorescent probe, Tyrosine-GQDs for the detection of SSRI drugs. Upon addition of SSRI drugs into the Tyrosine-GQDs, the fluorescence emission intensity of Tyrosine-GQDs decreased due to the complex formation through H-bonding which is evidenced by the rise of new peaks in fluorescence spectra and lifetime measurements. The calculated detection limit proves that the designed nanoprobe is highly sensitive and selective towards escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine, and fluoxetine are 0.13 × 10−6 M, 0.15 × 10−6 M, 0.07 × 10−6 M, and 0.07 × 10−6 M, respectively. A paper-based fluorescent sensor is fabricated using the designed probe which is efficient in the detection of the dosage of SSRI drugs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Changes in a physical, emotional, or psychological strain called stress are most common in recent days among all age groups. Right from teenage to old age people are experiencing an imbalance in their emotional state [1,2]. A decade ago, many people had never heard of the word stress, but today it is one of us and common to experience, just like a typical cold, cough, or headache. The outrageous fact is that children of the adolescent age group and youngsters are susceptible to the worst effects of stress, anxiety, and depression. The symptoms include depression, anxiety, chronic pain, mood swings, fatigue, indigestion, difficulty in breathing, panic attacks, etc., To treat these symptoms, antidepressants are given to the one who is suffering from stress. These drugs help in balancing the imbalanced neurotransmitters in the brain which communicate with each other. The antidepressants are classified based on their functionalities as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) and noradrenaline and specific serotoninergic antidepressants (NaSSAs). Each antidepressant works differently, by balancing the amount of serotonin and norepinephrine produced in the body.

Although a variety of antidepressants are available, doctors' first prescription would be SSRIs to treat stress and anxiety. The class SSRI antidepressant drugs mainly include escitalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine, and sertraline commonly known as Lexapro, Prozac or Sarafem, Paxil, and Zoloft respectively. The chemical structures of the SSRI drugs are given in Table 1 . SSRI drugs are witnessed as the first-line medications for treating major depressive disorders [3]. The usage of these drugs is wide in the medical domain to treat depression and stress. However, certain side effects are also associated with these drugs which are dependent on the dosage and can induce serotonergic effects. Nausea, sexual dysfunction, bleeding, sweating, skin problems, and gastrointestinal problems are commonly noticed side effects of SSRI drugs along with their effect on [3,4]. According to studies and reports, SSRI and SNRI medications are used to treat individuals with tension headaches and migraines [5].

When used in higher doses, SSRI medications pose a silent threat to society. Since depressed individuals frequently experience mood fluctuations, higher dosages taken consciously or unknowingly have negative effects on their health. The detection of high dosages is highly essential to the patient before the complications can become severe. The detection techniques for SSRI drugs are very minimal and none of them have been efficiently used in practice. Table 2 represents the lists of various materials and methods used for the detection of SSRI drugs along with the limit of detection as well as quantification. From Table 2, it is very clear that the latest nanomaterials haven't yet been used for the detection of SSRI drugs. Further, the conventional techniques used for SSRI drug quantifications involve a longer time for sample preparation, prolonged analysis time, cost, and lack of sensitivity. One of the major concerns with the conventional methods is they are not portable. Table 3 summarizes the list of conventional methods and their limits. The literature review makes it abundantly evident that just a small number of materials that can be counted are employed for SSRI drug detection. Fascinatingly, our synthesized nanomaterial (Tyrosine-GQDs) may be a useful novel material for the rapid detection of SSRI drugs.

A wide range of nanomaterials have been utilized for sensing various analytes including gases, metal ions, biological health markers, environmental pollutants, etc., Especially the footprints of nanomaterials and their impact are high in healthcare. There is no domain left out or untouched by the usage of nanomaterials and their applications. Although there are innumerable different types of nanoparticles available, carbon-based nanomaterials have a significant and unchallengeable position in the field of material science owing to their unique fluorescence behavior, biocompatibility, electronic properties, and many more [36]. The classifications based on size and properties are everywhere, similarly, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanoribbons (CNRs), fullerenes, and many more. Each carbon nanomaterial has high potential and has its importance in multiple domains.

GQDs are the nanoscale dimensions of a few layers of graphene stacked. GQDs are highly biocompatible and fluorescent. The characteristic properties of GQDs can be deliberately studied. The functionalization of GQDs with a variety of functional moieties adds up to their wings of significant characteristics and a variety of applications [37–42]. The importance of functionalized GQDs in the field of healthcare and diagnostics is of high importance [43–46]. GQDs are functionalized with tyrosine (an amino acid), via the in-situ functionalization method. In-situ functionalization is one of the easiest, cost and energy-efficient methods for functionalization. In our previous work, we have reported the synthetic method of tyrosine-GQDs and also characterization results [47]. In the current research, the effectiveness of in-situ functionalized Tyrosine-GQDs to quantitatively detect the SSRI drugs individually. The effect of interference and time, are analyzed using photoluminescence spectrophotometry. The detection limit for each of the drugs is quantified by the regression analysis using the data from fluorescence titration of drug vs. Tyrosine-GQDs. The paper-based sensor for the detection of SSRI drugs is efficiently designed and the fluorescence response is recorded.

L-Tyrosine, citric acid monohydrate are purchased from Sigma Aldrich (India), Escitalopram Oxalate tablets (10 mg), Sertraline tablet IP (25 mg), Paroxetine tablets IP (10 mg), and Fluoxetine capsules IP (10 mg) commercially known as C-Pram S 10, Serta 25, Pari 10 and Fludac 10 respectively are purchased from a nearby pharmacy. A thin layer chromatography (TLC) sheet is purchased from Merck (India). For the synthesis and solvent preparations, deionized water is used. All the drugs are initially dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water and then diluted to the concentration of 30 × 10−6 M. Tyrosine-GQDs dispersed in deionized water, (10 × 10−6 M) are used for the experiments. The host molecule and analyte concentration are fixed in the ratio of 1:3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of tyrosine functionalized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: L-tyrosine, as well as citric acid, are measured, and combined in a 100 mL beaker at an equal molar ratio. The beaker is heated at 200 °C. After 25 min, the mixture starts to slowly melt and gradually turns dark brown. At this point, 20 mL of deionized water is gradually added while being vigorously stirred at a velocity of 500 rpm to dissolve the Tyrosine-GQDs that have been produced in an aqueous solution. A stock solution is prepared based on the calculated concentration of the resulting dispersion. Fig. 1 represents the schematics of the synthesis method. Tyr-GQDs are analyzed through various important characterization techniques to study their characteristics which include XPS (ULVAC-PHI, PHI5000 Version Probe III), FTIR (Bruker Alpha – T, Germany), XRD (source λ - 1.542 A°, X'pert PRO PANalytical instrument, The Netherlands), and absorption and emission spectra (Agilent Technologies Spectro fluorophotometer G9800A at 298 K). Further, the detailed results of the characterization techniques including FESEM, and HRTEM, can be found in our previous report [47].

Upon functionalization of tyrosine with GQDs, tyrosine can contribute to the fluorescence of the GQDs due to the presence of O and N atoms. The designed Tyrosine-GQDs are fluorescent with an emission wavelength of 491 nm upon exciting at 398 nm (Fig. SI 1). XRD analysis demonstrates the crystallinity of tyrosine-GQDs (Fig. 2a). The functional groups of Tyrosine GQDs are verified using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). The XPS analysis evidences the formation of Tyrosine-GQDs through the survey spectrum, high-resolution spectra of C, N, and O (Fig. 2c-f). Tyr-GQDs have a fluorescence quantum yield of 0.50. Tyrosine-GQDs are 14–15 nm in size when measured in FESEM and HRTEM. The results of FESEM, HRTEM, and detailed characterization results are discussed in our previous research article [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of the limit of detection, the limit of quantitation, and the quenching rate constant
ce:section.ce:para:
The limit of detection and quantification of each SSRI drug is calculated with the help of regression analysis, Eqs. (1) and (2) respectively, in which σ is the standard deviation, and the slope value is obtained in regression analysis [51].
(1)
LoD =
3.3 ∗ σ
Slope
(2)
LoQ =
10 ∗ σ
Slope
To calculate the quenching rate constant and to plot the regression curve for Tyrosine-GQDs when bonded with SSRI drugs, the following Stern–Volmer Eq. (3) is used:
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
Q
where, F0 and F are fluorescence emission intensity before and after the addition of the drug, respectively. Q is the concentration of the SSRI drug. KSV represents the Stern–Volmer quenching rate constant. The slope obtained from the plot F0/F vs. [Q] gives the K SV value. Fig. 6a, b, c, and d show the plot of F0/F vs. [Q] using which the Stern–Volmer quenching rate constant is calculated for the C, S, P, and F drugs respectively. The working concentration range used for LoD and LoQ quantifications is listed in Table 5.

To study the efficacy of binding efficiency of Tyrosine-GQDs with the SSRI drugs, interference studies are carried out. The changes in the fluorescence spectra of the complexes Tyrosine-GQDs∙Esci, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Serta, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Pari, and Tyrosine-GQDs∙Flu are examined by the addition of other SSRI drugs into the complex (Fig. 7 ). It is evident from the fluorescence spectra recorded for each of the complexes formed between the Tyrosine-GQDs and the drugs that even after the addition of the other three SSRI drugs separately, the fluorescence spectra are unaffected. The complexes formed between the Tyrosine-GQDs and the first drug are very strong.

Fluorescence emission spectra are recorded in order to analyze i) the minimum time required for the interaction between Tyrosine-GQDs and SSRI drugs and ii) the stability of the complex formed with respect to time. The changes in the emission spectra of the Tyrosine-GQDs after the addition of the drug are recorded from 0 min to 60 min in different intervals. The minimum time required for the interaction between the Tyrosine-GQDs and SSRI drugs is less than a minute i.e., as soon as the drug is in contact with the Tyrosine-GQDs the complex is formed (Fig. 8 ). However, it is interesting to note that in Fig. 8d Tyrosine-GQDs∙Flu complex is regaining fluorescence at a slow pace which is either due to the structural or spatial arrangements of the fluoxetine drug where other complexes do not regain the fluorescence.

Fig. 9a, b, c, and d are the fluorescence spectra recorded with respect to the increase in time for the complexes Tyrosine-GQDs∙Esci, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Serta, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Pari, and Tyrosine-GQDs∙Flu to study the stability of the complexes formed. Once the complexes Tyrosine-GQDs∙Esci, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Serta, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Pari, are formed they are stable and there is no change in fluorescence intensity observed even after 1 h. However, a slight increase in fluorescence emission intensity is observed for the complex Tyrosine-GQDs∙Flu with an increase in time (Fig. 9d) which may be due to the spatial and structural rearrangement.

To study the practical application of drug sensing, the samples are analyzed using TLC paper decorated with Tyrosine GQDs under sunlight and UV light [52]. Initially, the TLC paper is dipped in Tyrosine-GQDs and dried at room temperature. Once the paper is dried different concentrations of the same volume (10 μL) of each drug are directly dropped onto the Tyrosine-GQDs coated TLC paper. On observing the papers in sunlight there is no change. Whereas in a UV lamp as the concentration decreased the luminescence of the sample decreased which demonstrates that the Tyrosine-GQDs are efficient in sensing, the drugs under a UV lamp by differentiating the changes in the color of the sample and luminescence behavior (Fig. 10 ). The designed paper-based sensor works in the concentration range of 30 μM to 1.89 μM. The experimented 5 different concentrations are 30, 15, 7.5, 3.75, and 1.89 μM (from left to right). The highest and lowest concentrations of 30 μM and 1.89 μM are the extreme ranges in which the sensor works.

In conclusion, we report the quantification of SSRI drugs, using Tyrosine-GQDs designed via the in-situ functionalization method. The four SSRI medications Esci, Serta, Pari, and Flu are successfully detected using a single fluorescent probe Tyrosine-GQDs. Although there aren't many studies on the subject, the ones that do exist are mainly based on HPLC and LCMS. However, material-based research for SSRI drug detection has just started. This study offers a significant contribution to the realm of drug sensitivity. The interaction between the Tyrosine-GQDs along with the drugs is confirmed by the formation of complexes through H-bonding, the existence of the isoemissive point, and new peaks observed in the fluorescence spectra recorded. To comprehend the stability of the complex produced over the remaining SSRI drugs and their impact on the complex, interference studies are conducted. The interference investigation demonstrates that once generated, the complexes are persistent and unaffected by further addition of drugs. Tyrosine-GQDs and the SSRI drugs can interact with each other in less than a minute time, and fluorescence spectrum analysis is also used to test the stability of the complex over time. It's interesting to note that only the Tyroisne-GQDs ∙ Flu combination can slowly restore fluorescence at a slow pace with the increase in time. The limit of detection and limit of quantification calculated from the fluorescence titration experiments for the interaction between the Tyrosine-GQDs with Esci, Serta, Pari, and Flu are 0.13 × 10−6 M, 0.15 × 10−6 M, 0.7 × 10−6 M, 0.07 × 10−6 M, and 0.4 × 10−6 M, 0.44 × 10−6 M, 0.22 × 10−6 M, 0.21 × 10−6 M, respectively for a range of working concentration. The LoD values depict that, out of 4 SSRI drugs, Paro can be detected in nanomolar concentration while others can be detected in micromolar concentration i.e., Tyrosine-GQDs are highly sensitive towards the Pari. The quenching rate constants for the complexes are calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation are 0.64 × 10−6 M−1, 0.6 × 10−6 M−1, 1.6 × 10−6 M−1 and 1.7 × 10−6 M−1 for the complexes Tyrosine-GQDs∙Esci, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Serta, Tyrosine-GQDs∙Pari, and Tyrosine-GQDs∙Flu correspondingly. Tyrosine-GQDs, a promising fluorescent probe, can also be used to practically determine the presence of SSRI medications in human bodily fluids with low detection limits. The fast-paced world poses a serious threat to anxiety, which can be brought on by social, familial, or self-pitying factors. Both the present and next generation will experience an increase in these factors. When depressed patients take their medications in excess dosage than the prescribed dosage, there finds an excellent practical application of the designed fluorescent probe. The fluorescent paper-based sensor is an easy and effective detection of SSRI drugs. The production of sensors for various biomarkers and biomolecules is expanding quickly, and soon a point-of-care device for the quantitative assessment of anti-depressants will be available on the market.

Pavithra Verthikere Ravi: Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Funding acquisition; Investigation; Methodology; Resources; Software; Validation; Visualization; Roles/Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing; Abirami Maharajan: Data curation; Investigation; Formal analysis; Validation; Ajay Pattabiraman: Resources; Investigation; Methodology; Formal analysis; Moorthi Pichumani: Resources; Investigation; Project administration; Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Pavithra V Ravi thanks the Council of Scientific & Industrial Research, India for the Senior Research Fellowship (08/757(0002)/2020EMR-I). The authors acknowledge The Management, SNR Sons Charitable Trust, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore, India for providing institutional research development fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110407.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Younger people are more vulnerable to stress, anxiety and depression during COVID-19 pandemic: a global cross-sectional survey
Prog. Neuro-Psychopharmacology Biol. Psychiatry.

Psychiatry. 109 (2021) 110236. doi:10.1016/j.pnpbp.2020.110236.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitation of escitalopram and its metabolites by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry in psychiatric patients: new metabolic ratio establishment

J. Chromatogr. Sci.

Chromatogr. B Anal. Technol. Biomed. Life Sci. 848 (2007) 391–394. doi:10.1016/j.jchromb.2006.10.058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive determination of fluoxetine and citalopram antidepressants in urine and wastewater samples by liquid chromatography coupled with photodiode array detector
